front: What is set associativity in cache memory?
back: Set associativity is a cache mapping technique that determines how main memory blocks are placed in cache. It defines the number of ways (positions) where a memory block can be placed within a cache set.
A cache set is a group of cache lines (ways) where a particular main memory block can be placed. If a cache is n-way set associative, each set contains n cache lines.
A cache tag is a portion of the main memory address used to identify which memory block is stored in a particular cache line. It is compared with the CPU address to determine a cache hit or miss.
Direct-mapped cache is a 1-way set associative cache where each memory block maps to exactly one cache line. It uses the formula: Set = (Block address) mod (Number of sets).
In fully associative cache, any memory block can be placed in any cache line. The entire address is treated as a tag, and all tags are searched in parallel for a match.

front: Cache address is decomposed into: Tag bits (identifies the memory block), Set index bits (identifies the cache set), and Block offset bits (identifies byte within the block).
back: Address = Tag + Set Index + Block Offset
Cache hit ratio = (Number of cache hits) / (Total memory accesses). A higher hit ratio indicates better cache performance.
Common replacement policies are: LRU (Least Recently Used), FIFO (First In First Out), and Random. LRU is most commonly used in set-associative caches.
Write-through writes data to both cache and main memory simultaneously. It ensures data consistency but increases memory traffic.
Write-back writes data only to cache initially, and main memory is updated only when the cache line is replaced. It reduces memory writes but requires dirty bit tracking.

front: What is the formula for number of sets in cache?
back: Number of sets = (Cache size) / (Block size × Associativity). For example, a 64KB cache with 64-byte blocks in 4-way set associative has 256 sets.

front: What is the advantage of higher associativity?
back: Higher associativity reduces conflict misses (fewer cache misses due to competing blocks) but increases hardware complexity, access time, and cost.
A conflict miss occurs in direct-mapped or set-associative cache when multiple memory blocks map to the same cache set and repeatedly evict each other.

front: What is the dirty bit in cache?
back: The dirty bit (or modified bit) indicates whether a cache line has been modified compared to main memory. It is used in write-back policy to determine if write-back to memory is needed.
Miss penalty is the additional time required to fetch data from main memory when a cache miss occurs. It includes the time to access memory and transfer the block to cache.
